stem: A moped (bromfiets) in the Netherlands is identified by which licence-plate colour?

choices.text: Yellow
Blue
Green
White

explanation.short: A bromfiets (up to 45 km/h) carries a yellow plate; a snorfiets (25 km/h) carries a blue plate.
explanation.examTip: Plate colour tells you the rider's top speed — a yellow plate closes on you faster than a blue one.

stem: How much space should you leave when overtaking a cyclist?

choices.text: At least 1.5 metres
About 30 centimetres
Exactly half a metre
No minimum, as long as you sound the horn

explanation.short: Leave at least 1.5 metres; if the road is too narrow to do so, wait.
explanation.examTip: A cyclist may wobble or swerve around a pothole, so generous space protects you both.

stem: You want to turn right, but a cyclist on the cycle path beside you is going straight on. What must you do?

choices.text: Give way and let the cyclist pass before turning
Turn quickly in front of the cyclist to clear the junction
Sound the horn so the cyclist stops for you
Speed up because vehicles always have priority over cyclists

explanation.short: A vehicle turning off must give way to traffic continuing straight on the path it crosses.
explanation.examTip: Check your right mirror and blind spot before turning — this prevents the classic right-hook crash.

stem: You are driving past a row of parked cars with a cyclist ahead of you on the same road. What is the main danger to anticipate?

choices.text: A car door opening into the cyclist's path
The parked cars rolling forward
The cyclist suddenly reversing
Nothing — parked cars pose no risk

explanation.short: An opening door can force the cyclist to swerve into your path, so give parked cars a wide berth.
explanation.examTip: Watch for people sitting in parked cars; their presence is a warning a door may open.

stem: What is the maximum design speed of a snorfiets (light moped, blue plate)?

choices.text: 25 km/h
45 km/h
60 km/h
15 km/h

explanation.short: A snorfiets is limited to 25 km/h; a bromfiets (yellow plate) may reach 45 km/h.
explanation.examTip: Knowing the speed helps you judge how quickly a two-wheeler will reach the junction.

stem: You check your mirror before turning right and see no cyclist, yet one is actually riding up close on your inside. Why might the mirror have missed them?

choices.text: The cyclist was in your blind spot, which the mirror cannot show
Cyclists are never visible in mirrors
The mirror only shows vehicles with engines
Mirrors do not work at junctions

explanation.short: A two-wheeler can sit exactly in the blind spot the mirror cannot cover, so a shoulder check is needed.
explanation.examTip: Always add a quick glance over your shoulder before turning across a cycle path.

stem: A pedestrian is clearly waiting to step onto a marked zebra crossing ahead of you. What must you do?

choices.text: Give way and let them cross
Continue, because waiting pedestrians have no priority
Only stop if they make eye contact first
Speed up to clear the crossing before they step out

explanation.short: At a zebra you must give way to pedestrians crossing or clearly waiting to cross.
explanation.examTip: Approach every zebra at a speed that lets you stop in time.

stem: A pedestrian at the kerb is holding a white cane with red bands. What does this tell you?

choices.text: The person is deaf-blind and may not see or hear you — give them time
The person is a traffic warden
The cane marks a temporary road closure
You should sound your horn to alert them

explanation.short: A white cane (with red bands for deaf-blind) marks a vulnerable pedestrian; stop and give them time.
explanation.examTip: Never sound the horn at them — it can startle and disorient rather than help.

stem: You enter a woonerf (home zone) marked with the white house symbol. How should you drive?

choices.text: At walking pace, giving way to pedestrians who may use the full street
At the normal 50 km/h built-up limit
As fast as the road surface allows
At 30 km/h, ignoring pedestrians on the road

explanation.short: In a woonerf you drive at walking pace; pedestrians and playing children may use the whole width.
explanation.examTip: The white house sign is the cue — treat the entire street as shared space.

stem: Approaching a zebra crossing, the car in the lane next to you has stopped though you cannot see why. What should you do?

choices.text: Stop too, because it may be yielding to a pedestrian hidden from your view
Overtake it quickly while the crossing looks clear
Sound your horn so it moves on
Continue at the same speed in your own lane

explanation.short: Never pass a vehicle stopped at a zebra; it may be giving way to a pedestrian you cannot see.
explanation.examTip: Only continue once you can see for yourself that the crossing is clear.

stem: On a road with no pavement, where should pedestrians normally walk?

choices.text: On the carriageway facing oncoming traffic
In the middle of the lane
On the side with their back to traffic
Pedestrians are forbidden on such roads

explanation.short: Without a pavement, pedestrians walk facing oncoming traffic so they can see approaching vehicles.
explanation.examTip: As a driver, expect people walking toward you on the edge of unpaved or rural roads.

stem: Why do children require extra caution as pedestrians near the road?

choices.text: They may run out suddenly without checking for traffic
They always have right of way everywhere
They can hear cars from much further away
They never cross roads alone

explanation.short: Children can act unpredictably and run into the road, so reduce speed and stay ready to stop.
explanation.examTip: Near schools, playgrounds and ice-cream vans, treat any child as likely to step out.

stem: At an uncontrolled meeting with no signs or signals, who generally has priority — you or an approaching tram?

choices.text: The tram, because it cannot steer around you and needs a long distance to stop
You, because cars always have priority over rail vehicles
Whoever is travelling faster
Neither — both must stop completely

explanation.short: A tram generally has priority where no signs or signals decide otherwise.
explanation.examTip: At signed or signalled junctions the normal rules apply to the tram as well.

stem: Inside a built-up area, a bus signals to pull away from a marked bus stop. What must you do?

choices.text: Give way and let the bus pull out, slowing if necessary
Keep going, the bus must always wait for you
Overtake on the passenger side to get past quickly
Stop and wait until all passengers have left the area

explanation.short: Inside built-up areas you must give way to a bus signalling to leave a stop.
explanation.examTip: Outside built-up areas the bus does not get this priority — read the location first.

stem: You reach a railway level crossing where the red lights are flashing and the barriers are coming down. What must you do?

choices.text: Stop and wait; never zig-zag around the barriers
Cross quickly before the barriers fully close
Steer around the half-closed barriers if no train is visible
Stop only if you can actually see a train

explanation.short: Flashing red lights or lowering barriers mean stop and wait — going round them is forbidden.
explanation.examTip: A second train may follow; only cross once the lights are off and barriers fully up.

stem: A tram driver sees a special signal showing a horizontal white bar. What does this mean for the tram?

choices.text: Stop
Proceed at full speed
Sound the bell and continue
Reverse out of the junction

explanation.short: On the tram's white-bar signals a horizontal bar means stop; a vertical or diagonal bar means go.
explanation.examTip: Recognising tram signals helps you predict whether a tram will stop or move at a junction.

stem: Your engine stalls while you are stopped on a railway level crossing and a train is approaching. What is the safest action?

choices.text: Get everyone out of the vehicle and move well clear of the crossing
Stay in the car and keep trying to restart the engine
Stand beside the car and wave at the train
Push the car backwards onto the rails behind you

explanation.short: Your life matters more than the vehicle — leave it and get clear of the tracks immediately.
explanation.examTip: Never stop on a crossing unless you are certain you can clear it completely first.

stem: You are passing a stationary bus at a stop. What is the key hazard to watch for?

choices.text: Pedestrians crossing in front of or behind the bus
The bus suddenly reversing into you
The bus changing colour
Nothing, a stopped bus is never a hazard

explanation.short: Passengers may step out from in front of or behind the bus where you cannot see them.
explanation.examTip: Never overtake a bus on the side where passengers board or alight.

stem: Which combination of signals means an emergency vehicle is claiming priority and you must clear its path?

choices.text: Blue flashing lights together with a siren
Blue flashing lights only, with no siren
Headlights flashed once
Hazard warning lights

explanation.short: Both blue lights and a siren together mean the vehicle has priority and you must make room.
explanation.examTip: Blue lights without a siren mean it is not currently claiming priority — stay alert but you need not give way.

stem: On a multi-lane road, how should drivers form a path for an approaching emergency vehicle?

choices.text: Left-lane drivers move left, right-lane drivers move right, leaving a gap down the middle
Everyone moves into the left lane
Everyone brakes hard and stops where they are
Everyone drives onto the hard shoulder

explanation.short: This forms a rescue corridor down the centre so the emergency vehicle can pass.
explanation.examTip: Do not use the hard shoulder unless specifically directed to.

stem: You are approaching a horse and rider on a country road. How should you pass?

choices.text: Slowly, widely and quietly, without sounding the horn
Quickly so you spend less time alongside
While sounding the horn to warn the horse
By revving the engine to make the horse move aside

explanation.short: A startled horse is dangerous, so pass slowly, give plenty of room and keep noise down.
explanation.examTip: If someone is leading animals across the road, wait until they are clear.

stem: A police officer is directing traffic and signals you to stop, but the traffic light ahead is green. What do you do?

choices.text: Stop — the officer's instructions override the signal
Drive on, because a green light always takes priority
Stop only if other cars stop first
Ignore the officer and follow the road markings

explanation.short: A traffic controller's directions override signs, signals and the normal rules.
explanation.examTip: Temporary signs and instructions always take priority over permanent ones.

stem: An escorted military or funeral convoy is passing through a junction. What should you do?

choices.text: Treat it as one long vehicle and wait until it has passed
Drive into a gap between the vehicles when one appears
Overtake the whole convoy as fast as possible
Sound the horn to make them speed up

explanation.short: Do not break into an escorted convoy; treat it as a single vehicle and let it pass.
explanation.examTip: An escort vehicle may direct you to stop, and you must obey.

stem: You are first at a red light when an ambulance comes up behind with blue lights and siren. What is the correct response?

choices.text: Move forward past the stop line only if it is safe, to clear a path, then let it pass
Stay still at the red light no matter what
Accelerate away at full speed to get out of the area
Reverse to make space behind you

explanation.short: The duty to give way to a genuine priority vehicle outweighs the red signal, done safely at walking pace.
explanation.examTip: Only move when no one is endangered — never lurch blindly into the junction.

stem: A snorfiets (blue licence plate, maximum 25 km/h) reaches a stretch of road that has a cycle path alongside it. What must the rider do?

choices.text: Use the carriageway, because all mopeds are motor vehicles and must use the road
Use the cycle path, because snorfietsen must use the cycle path where one is available
Use either — the rider may choose whichever is more convenient
Use the cycle path only if a speed sign shows less than 30 km/h

explanation.short: In the Netherlands, snorfietsen (blue plate, max 25 km/h) must use the cycle path where one is available — they may not use the carriageway.
explanation.examTip: Snorfiets (blue plate) = cycle path. Bromfiets (yellow plate, max 45 km/h) = carriageway. The plate colour tells you the rule.

stem: A cyclist is already on a Dutch roundabout crossing the exit you want to use to leave. Who has priority?

choices.text: You — you are in a car and leaving the roundabout
The cyclist — they are already on the roundabout and have priority over exiting vehicles
You — cars always have priority over cyclists on roundabouts
Whoever reaches the exit road first

explanation.short: A cyclist circulating on a roundabout has priority over a car leaving it. The give-way markings at roundabout exits confirm this — yield to all circulating traffic, including cyclists.
explanation.examTip: On many modern Dutch roundabouts, cycle paths cross each exit. Where a cycle path crosses your exit, the cyclist on that path also has priority.

image.alt: Plan view of a roundabout where a cyclist already circulating crosses the exit you want to take; the exiting car must give way.
image.caption: A circulating cyclist has priority over a car leaving the roundabout

stem: You are on a country road and want to pass a horse and rider. The rider signals you to wait. What should you do?

choices.text: Sound the horn gently to warn the horse you are there
Pass quickly to minimise the time the horse is near traffic
Wait until the rider signals it is safe to pass, then pass slowly giving as much space as possible
Move to the centre of the road and pass at 30 km/h

explanation.short: Horses can spook suddenly from engine noise or quick movements. Always wait for the rider's signal, then pass very slowly — at walking pace if needed — with as much lateral space as possible.
explanation.examTip: Never sound your horn near a horse. Slow, quiet, and wide is the rule. A startled horse can cause serious injury to both rider and driver.

stem: You arrive at an intersection at the same time as a tram coming from your left. There are no lights or signs. Who must yield?

choices.text: The tram — it is on your left, so you have right of way
You — trams always have priority over motor vehicles at intersections
Whoever arrived at the intersection first
The tram, if it is moving faster than you are

explanation.short: Trams have priority over all motor vehicles at intersections, regardless of which direction they approach from. This rule overrides the normal right-of-way principle.
explanation.examTip: Do not apply the right-has-priority rule to trams — trams always win at intersections.

stem: A person pushing a pram is waiting at a zebra crossing and is about to step onto the road. Are they entitled to the same right of way as any other pedestrian?

choices.text: No — only walking pedestrians have zebra-crossing rights
No — a pram counts as a vehicle
Yes — a person pushing a pram is a pedestrian and has full right of way at a zebra crossing
Only if the pram is not wider than 80 cm

explanation.short: A person pushing a pram, wheelchair, or mobility device is classified as a pedestrian. They have the same right of way at a zebra crossing as anyone on foot.
explanation.examTip: Wheelchair users, people with pushchairs, and those using walking aids all count as pedestrians for right-of-way purposes.

stem: You indicate to turn right. A cyclist is in the cycle lane to your right, travelling straight on at the same speed. You cannot see them in your mirrors. What should you do?

choices.text: Turn right — your indicator is on and the cyclist must yield to a turning vehicle
Check your right blind spot, let the cyclist pass, then turn
Accelerate slightly to turn before the cyclist draws level
Sound the horn so the cyclist knows you are turning

explanation.short: A cyclist going straight on in a cycle lane always has priority over a car turning right. Check the right blind spot, confirm the cyclist has passed, and only then turn.
explanation.examTip: The right hook — turning right across an unseen cyclist — is one of the most common fatal cycling accidents. Always check the right blind spot last, immediately before turning.
